title: Charmed Life - Chapter 1 (Fragment 1)
text:
Chapter 1

1

C
 AT C HANT ADMIRED his elder sister Gwendolen. She was a witch. He admired her and he clung to her. Great changes came about in their lives and left him no one else to cling to.

The first great change came about when their parents took them out for a day trip down the river in a paddle steamer. They set out in great style, Gwendolen and her mother in white dresses with ribbons, Cat and his father in prickly blue-serge Sunday suits. It was a hot day. The steamer was crammed with other people in holiday clothes, talking, laughing, eating whelks with thin slices of white bread and butter, while the paddleboat steam organ wheezed out popular tunes so that no one could hear themselves talk.

In fact the steamer was too crowded and too old. Something went wrong with the steering. The whole laughing, whelk-eating, Sunday-dressed crowd was swept away in the current from the dam. They hit one of the posts which were supposed to stop people being swept away, and the paddle steamer, being old, simply broke into pieces. Cat remembered the organ playing and the paddles beating the blue sky. Clouds of steam screamed from broken pipes and drowned the screams from the crowd, as every single person aboard was swept away through the dam. It was a terrible accident. The papers called it the Saucy Nancy Disaster. The ladies in their clinging skirts were quite unable to swim. The men in tight blue serge were very little better off. But Gwendolen was a witch, so she could not drown. And Cat, who flung his arms around Gwendolen when the boat hit the post, survived too. There were very few other survivors.

The whole country was shocked by it. The paddleboat company and the town of Wolvercote between them paid for the funerals. Gwendolen and Cat were given heavy black clothes at public expense, and rode behind the procession of hearses in a carriage pulled by black horses with black plumes on their heads. The other survivors rode with them. Cat looked at them and wondered if they were witches and warlocks, but he never found out. The Mayor of Wolvercote had set up a Fund for the survivors. Money poured in from all over the country. All the other survivors took their share and went away to start new lives elsewhere. Only Cat and Gwendolen were left and, since nobody could discover any of their relations, they stayed in Wolvercote.

They became celebrities for a time. Everyone was very kind. Everyone said what beautiful little orphans they were. It was true. They were both fair and pale, with blue eyes, and looked good in black. Gwendolen was very pretty, and tall for her age. Cat was small for his age. Gwendolen was very motherly to Cat, and people were touched. Cat did not mind. It made up a little for the empty, lost way he was feeling. Ladies gave him cake and toys. Town Councillors came and asked how he was getting on; and the Mayor called and patted him on the head. The Mayor explained that the money from the Fund was being put into a Trust for them until they were grown up. Meanwhile, the town would pay for their education and upbringing.

“And where would you little people like to live?” he asked kindly.

Gwendolen at once said that old Mrs. Sharp downstairs had offered to take them in. “She’s been ever so kind to us,” she explained. “We’d love to live with her.”

vocabulary.definition: Regarded with respect or warm approval.
vocabulary.example: CATCHANT ADMIRED his elder sister Gwendolen.

vocabulary.definition: Famous people, often admired by the public.
vocabulary.example: They became celebrities for a time.

vocabulary.definition: Tight-fitting or adhering closely to the body or object.
vocabulary.example: The ladies in their clinging skirts were quite unable to swim.

vocabulary.definition: Held on tightly to something or someone.
vocabulary.example: He admired her and he clung to her.

vocabulary.definition: Filled to capacity; packed tightly.
vocabulary.example: The steamer was crammed with other people in holiday clothes.

vocabulary.definition: A large number of people gathered together.
vocabulary.example: The whole laughing, whelk-eating, Sunday-dressed crowd was swept away in the current.

vocabulary.definition: A flow of water in a definite direction, especially through a surrounding body of water.
vocabulary.example: Swept away in the current from the dam.

vocabulary.definition: A barrier built to hold back water and raise its level.
vocabulary.example: They hit one of the posts which were supposed to stop people being swept away through the dam.

vocabulary.definition: A sudden event, such as an accident or a natural catastrophe, causing great damage or loss of life.
vocabulary.example: The papers called it the Saucy Nancy Disaster.

vocabulary.definition: Die through submersion in and inhalation of water.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen was a witch, so she could not drown.

vocabulary.definition: A sum of money saved or made available for a particular purpose.
vocabulary.example: The Mayor of Wolvercote had set up a Fund for the survivors.

vocabulary.definition: Ceremonies held for burying or cremating the dead.
vocabulary.example: They paid for the funerals.

vocabulary.definition: Vehicles for conveying the dead to the place of burial.
vocabulary.example: The procession of hearses in a carriage pulled by black horses.

vocabulary.definition: Having qualities befitting a mother, especially in kindness and care.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen was very motherly to Cat.

vocabulary.definition: A child whose parents are dead.
vocabulary.example: Everyone said what beautiful little orphans they were.

vocabulary.definition: A steamship propelled by a paddle wheel or wheels.
vocabulary.example: They took them out for a day trip down the river in a paddle steamer.

vocabulary.definition: Feathers or arrangements of feathers on a helmet, hat, or plume of smoke or dust.
vocabulary.example: Black horses with black plumes on their heads.

vocabulary.definition: A number of people or vehicles moving forward as part of a ceremony or festival.
vocabulary.example: They rode behind the procession of hearses in a carriage.

vocabulary.definition: Uttered a loud, sharp, piercing cry.
vocabulary.example: Clouds of steam screamed from broken pipes and drowned the screams from the crowd.

vocabulary.definition: A ship propelled by steam.
vocabulary.example: The steamer was too crowded and too old.

vocabulary.definition: The act or mechanism of guiding or controlling the direction of a vehicle or vessel.
vocabulary.example: Something went wrong with the steering.

vocabulary.definition: People who continue to live after an event in which others died.
vocabulary.example: There were very few other survivors.

vocabulary.definition: Emotionally moved or affected.
vocabulary.example: People were touched by Gwendolen's care for Cat.

vocabulary.definition: An arrangement where money or property is held by one party for the benefit of another.
vocabulary.example: The money from the Fund was being put into a Trust for them.

vocabulary.definition: The treatment and instruction received by a child from its parents throughout its childhood.
vocabulary.example: The town would pay for their education and upbringing.

vocabulary.definition: Edible sea snails commonly eaten as seafood.
vocabulary.example: Eating whelks with thin slices of white bread and butter.
